deals_cleared.merger_name: Life Without Barriers – Mallacoota District Health & Support Service

targets.name: MALLACOOTA DISTRICT HEALTH & SUPPORT SERVICE INC

deals_cleared.merger_description: Life Without Barriers (**Life Without Barriers**), an ACNC-registered charity incorporated as a public company limited by guarantee, proposes to merge with Mallacoota District Health & Support Services Inc. (**MDHSS**), an ACNC registered charity incorporated as a Victorian incorporated association. By way of a Merger Implementation Deed, Life Without Barriers, together with five Life Without Barriers nominees (comprising directors and/or executives of Life Without Barriers), propose to become the only members of MDHSS, replacing all current members of MDHSS (the **Acquisition**).

events.title: Notification waiver determination published
events.determination_commission_division: Determination made by Commissioner Williams pursuant to a delegation under section 25(1) of the Act

determination_table_content.details:
Life Without Barriers applied for a notification waiver in respect of its
proposed acquisition of Mallacoota District Health & Support
Services Inc. (MDHSS), as described in the transaction documents
provided as part of the application (the Acquisition).

The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has
determined under section 51ABV(1)(a) of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) that the Acquisition is not required to be
notified.
17 June 2026

Life Without Barriers, is a not-for-profit organisation that provides
comprehensive social services to communities across Australia.
Relevantly, it is an NDIS-registered provider of disability services. It
also provides in-home aged care services, providing independent
living and behavioural support to elderly clients and persons with
disabilities. These services are provided in various locations
throughout Australia.
MDHSS, provides primary and allied health, aged care, disability and
community services in Mallacoota and surrounding areas.
Relevantly, MDHSS’ aged care services offering includes both in-
home care for elderly clients, as well as operating independent living
units and a respite care centre. As an unregistered disability support
provider, its disability services are limited to a variety of everyday
disability supports, provided the participant’s funding is self-
managed or plan-managed.
MDHSS also operates a small medical centre in Mallacoota from
which primary and allied health services are provided to Mallacoota
residents. Many of these services (including podiatry, physiotherapy,
pathology, dentistry and psychology) are supplied by independent
contractors operating within MDHSS’ medical centre.
Mallacoota is a town in the East Gippsland region in Victoria,
Australia. Life Without Barriers does not offer services within the
East Gippsland region of Victoria.

In making this notification waiver determination, the Australian
Competition and Consumer Commission (the ACCC) has considered
the information provided with the notification waiver application and
had regard to the factors in section 51ABV(2)(b) of the Competition
and Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) (the Act).
Based on the information provided in the application, the ACCC
considers that the Acquisition is unlikely to give rise to any material
lessening of competition. In particular:
a. there is no horizontal overlap between Life Without Barriers
and MDHSS in the supply of in-home aged care and disability
services in Mallacoota and surrounding areas and a small
horizontal overlap in the wider regional area
b. there are alternative suppliers of in-home aged care and
disability services in Mallacoota and surrounding areas.
In these circumstances, the ACCC does not consider it necessary to
reach a concluded view on the likelihood of the notification
thresholds being met.
Given that material competition concerns are unlikely to arise, the
ACCC has determined that the Acquisition is not required to be
notified.
The ACCC considers that the determination is consistent with the
object of the Act and the interests of consumers in promoting
competition.
For more information about the ACCC’s approach to considering
notification waiver applications and to assessing competition
effects more generally, see the ACCC’s interim guidance on
notification waivers and merger assessment guidelines.

events.title: Stonepeak - Castrol - Phase 1 Determination

determination_table_content.details:
The Acquisition comprises:
• the acquisition of 100% of the shares in Castrol Group
Holdings Limited (Castrol) by Stonepeak Partners LP
(together with its affiliates, Stonepeak) from BP p.l.c. (BP),
through Motion JVCo Limited (Motion JVCo), a special
purpose vehicle wholly owned by a company which is
indirectly owned by investment funds advised and/or
managed by Stonepeak,
• the reinvestment by BP, Castrol’s current owner, in Castrol at
closing by acquiring 35% of the shares in Motion JVCo,
through a wholly owned subsidiary. Stonepeak will hold the
remaining 65% of Motion JVCo, and
• the indirect investment by Canada Pension Plan Investment
Board (CPPIB) in Motion JVCo, through its wholly owned
subsidiary CPP Investment Board Private Holdings (6) Inc,
which is expected to occur through an investment in another
Stonepeak entity.

The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has
determined under section 51ABZE(1) of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) that the Acquisition may be put into effect.

Castrol is BP’s global lubricants business, headquartered in the
United Kingdom. Castrol is active globally in the development,
production and sale of lubricants and related fluids for various
industries including automotive, energy, industrial and marine.
BP is a global integrated energy group with operations in Europe,
North and South America, Australasia, Asia and Africa.
Stonepeak is a global investment firm specialising in infrastructure
and real estate assets.
CPPIB is a professional investment management organisation that
manages the Canada Pension Plan Fund (CPP Fund) in the best
interest of more than 22 million contributors and beneficiaries.

determination_table_content.item:
Overlap and relationship
between the parties
determination_table_content.details:
There is no horizontal overlap in Australia between Castrol’s
activities and the activities of either Stonepeak and its connected
entities, or CPPIB and its connected entities.
Neither Stonepeak (or its connected entities) or CPPIB (or its
connected entities) are material suppliers to or customers of Castrol
in Australia.

When making a determination in Phase 1, the Australian Competition
and Consumer Commission (ACCC) undertakes a competition
assessment and considers whether it is appropriate for an
acquisition to be approved or subject to further assessment in
Phase 2 in accordance with section 51ABZJ of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) (the Act). In doing so, the ACCC must have
regard to the object of the Act and all relevant matters, including the
interests of consumers.
For more information about the ACCC’s approach to considering
notified acquisitions, see the ACCC’s merger assessment guidelines
and interim merger process guidelines.
In conducting its competition assessment, the ACCC has considered
the information and documents that were submitted with the
notification form and publicly available information.
The ACCC has determined that the Acquisition may be put into
effect as it considers that the Acquisition is unlikely to have the
effect of substantially lessening competition in any market. In
reaching its decision, and based on the material before it, the ACCC
makes the following findings.
• There are no horizontal overlaps between Castrol and either of
Stonepeak or CPPIB (or their connected entities).
• Based on the information before us, there do not appear to be
material customer or supplier relationships between Castrol and
either of Stonepeak or CPPIB (or their connected entities), that
are likely to give rise to vertical or conglomerate competition
concerns in Australia.

A notifying party, or other person who has been allowed to do so by
the Australian Competition Tribunal, may apply for review if they are
dissatisfied with the determination. Pursuant to section 100C of the
Act, applications for review of the determination are to be made to
the Australian Competition Tribunal before the end of 14 calendar
days after this statement of reasons was included on the ACCC’s
Acquisitions Register. To confirm whether there has been any
application for review, please contact the Australian Competition
Tribunal.

events.title: Notification waiver determination published
events.determination_commission_division: Determination made by Commissioner Williams pursuant to a delegation under section 25(1) of the Act

determination_table_content.details:
Xylem Analytics Germany GmbH, a wholly owned subsidiary of
Xylem Inc. (together, Xylem), applied for a notification waiver in
respect of its proposed acquisition of 100% the shares in TriOS
Mess-und Datentechnik GmbH (including its subsidiaries in Australia,
Malaysia and New Zealand) (TriOS), as described in the transaction
documents provided as part of the application (the Acquisition).

The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has
determined under section 51ABV(1)(a) of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) that the Acquisition is not required to be
notified.
18 June 2026

The acquirer, Xylem, is a global water technology company
headquartered in Indiana, United States. It focuses on the design,
manufacture and service of engineered products and solutions
across a wide variety of critical applications, primarily in the water
sector. Its broad portfolio of products, services and solutions
address customer needs across the water cycle from the delivery,
treatment, measurement and use of drinking water, to the collection,
testing, analysis and treatment of wastewater, and finally the return
of water to the environment.
The target, TriOS, is a company headquartered in Rastede, Germany,
with sales and service operations in Australia, Malaysia and New
Zealand. It develops, manufactures and supplies optical
measurement and sensor systems, primarily for water monitoring.
Its portfolio includes optical sensors, such as photometers,
fluorometers, radiometers and electrochemical sensors that
measure physical, chemical and biological parameters including
nitrate, nitrite, organic carbon compounds, turbidity, oil content and
algal activity. In addition, TriOS offers controllers and integrated
measurement and system solutions.
In Australia, the parties both supply analytical instrumentation for
water quality used to measure, monitor and analyse the physical,
chemical and biological parameters of water in different
applications.

In making this notification waiver determination, the Australian
Competition and Consumer Commission (the ACCC) has considered
the information provided with the notification waiver application and
had regard to the factors in section 51ABV(2)(b) of the Competition
and Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) (the Act).
Based on the information currently before it, the ACCC considers that
the Acquisition is unlikely to give rise to any material lessening of
competition. In particular:
a. there is a small horizontal overlap in the supply of analytical
instrumentation for water quality in Australia, and the
increase in share of supply arising from the Acquisition
would be low
b. there are alternative suppliers of analytical instrumentation
for water quality in Australia
c. TriOS has a small presence in Australia.
The ACCC has also had regard to the likelihood that, if the
Acquisition were put into effect, the notification thresholds
determined under section 51ABP(1) of the Act would apply.
While the ACCC considers that the notification thresholds are likely
to be met, given that material competition concerns are unlikely to
arise, the ACCC has determined that the Acquisition is not required
to be notified.
The ACCC considers that the determination is consistent with the
object of the Act and the interests of consumers in promoting
competition.
For more information about the ACCC’s approach to considering
notification waiver applications and to assessing competition
effects more generally, see the ACCC’s interim guidance on
notification waivers and merger assessment guidelines.

determination_table_content.details:
Online Education Services Pty Ltd (OES) applied for a notification
waiver in respect of its proposed acquisition of 100% of the share
capital in South Australian Institute of Business and Technology Pty
Ltd (SAIBT), as described in the transaction documents provided as
part of the application (the Acquisition).

The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has
determined under section 51ABV(1)(a) of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) that the Acquisition is not required to be
notified.
18 June 2026

The acquirer, OES, provides end-to-end online education delivery
services to higher education providers such as universities. This
includes delivering online courses on behalf of higher education
providers. OES does not currently possess the regulatory approvals
necessary to provide these courses directly to students (other than
indirectly through Swinburne University, which owns 20% of OES –
with the remaining 80% owned by Seek Growth Nominees Pty Ltd).
The target, SAIBT, is part of the Navitas Group. SAIBT has historically
offered face-to-face diploma qualifications in Adelaide intended to
act as pathway qualifications to bachelor degrees offered by the
University of South Australia. Following the merger of the University
of South Australia and the University of Adelaide to form Adelaide
University, SAIBT commenced transitioning its students to Eynesbury
College, another entity in the Navitas Group. SAIBT has ceased
enrolling new students and will have no students at completion of
the Acquisition. As such, on completion, OES will acquire SAIBT as a
dormant ‘shell’ with regulatory approvals to operate higher education
diploma qualifications.

In making this notification waiver determination, the Australian
Competition and Consumer Commission (the ACCC) has considered
the information provided with the notification waiver application and
had regard to the factors in section 51ABV(2)(b) of the Competition
and Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) (the Act).
Based on the information currently before it, the ACCC considers that
the Acquisition is unlikely to give rise to any material lessening of
competition. In particular:
a. the Acquisition will not increase concentration in the supply
of diploma/pathway qualifications in Australia
b. the Acquisition will facilitate new entry by OES into the
supply of diploma/pathway qualifications in Australia
c. there are alternative suppliers of diploma/pathway
qualifications in Australia.
In these circumstances, the ACCC does not consider it necessary to
reach a concluded view on the likelihood of the notification
thresholds being met.
Given that material competition concerns are unlikely to arise, the
ACCC has determined that the Acquisition is not required to be
notified.
The ACCC considers that the determination is consistent with the
object of the Act and the interests of consumers in promoting
competition.
For more information about the ACCC’s approach to considering
notification waiver applications and to assessing competition
effects more generally, see the ACCC’s interim guidance on
notification waivers and merger assessment guidelines.

provide core and ancillary veterinary services primarily for household
pets as well as livestock and exotic species. The services primarily

Vets Central and Morley Veterinary Hospital both supply first opinion
veterinary services primarily for household pets, livestock and exotic

In making this notification waiver determination, the Australian
Competition and Consumer Commission (the ACCC) has considered
the information provided with the notification waiver application and
had regard to the factors in section 51ABV(2)(b) of the Competition
and Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) (the Act).
Based on the information currently before it, the ACCC considers that
the Acquisition is unlikely to give rise to any material lessening of
competition. In particular:
a. there is no horizontal overlap between Vets Central and
Morley Veterinary Hospital in the supply of veterinary
services in the Morley area
b. there are alternative suppliers of veterinary services in the
Morley area and more broadly at the, state and national
level.
The ACCC has also had regard to the likelihood that, if the
Acquisition were put into effect, the notification thresholds
determined under section 51ABP(1) of the Act would apply.
While the ACCC considers that the notification thresholds are likely
to be met, given that material competition concerns are unlikely to
arise, the ACCC has determined that the Acquisition is not required
to be notified.
The ACCC considers that the determination is consistent with the

deals_cleared.merger_name: Northstar – business assets of Mildura Mazda
deals_cleared.merger_description: Northstar Mildura Pty Ltd (**Northstar**) proposes to acquire certain business assets associated with the Mildura Mazda dealership.

events.title: Notification waiver determination published
events.determination_commission_division: Determination made by Commissioner Woodward pursuant to a delegation under section 25(1) of the Act

determination_table_content.details:
Northstar Mildura Pty Ltd (Northstar) applied for a notification
waiver in respect of its proposed acquisition of certain business
assets associated with the Mildura Mazda dealership, as described
in the transaction documents provided as part of the application (the
Acquisition).

The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has
determined under section 51ABV(1)(a) of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) that the Acquisition is not required to be
notified.
17 June 2026

The acquirer, Northstar, is part of an automotive group comprising a
number of related entities which own and operate motor vehicle
dealerships. Across those entities, the group is engaged in the retail
supply of motor vehicles, including new, demonstrator and used
vehicles, across a range of automotive brands (including Hyundai,
Nissan and BMW). The group also supplies aftermarket services,
including vehicle servicing, repairs, spare parts and accessories, and
may facilitate ancillary services such as vehicle finance, insurance
and extended warranties.
Mildura Mazda, conducts motor vehicle dealership activities,
including the retail and wholesale sale of new and used Mazda
vehicles, vehicle servicing and repairs, the sale of spare parts and
accessories, and associated aftersales services. The business
primarily services customers in Mildura and the surrounding regional
catchment, including north-west Victoria and adjoining regions of
New South Wales and South Australia.
Northstar and Mildura Mazda are both involved in the supply of new
and used cars, and associated goods and services, in the Mildura
region.

In making this notification waiver determination, the Australian
Competition and Consumer Commission (the ACCC) has considered
the information provided with the notification waiver application and
certain publicly available information, and had regard to the factors
in section 51ABV(2)(b) of the Competition and Consumer Act 2010
(Cth) (the Act).
Based on the information currently before it, the ACCC considers that
the Acquisition is unlikely to give rise to any material lessening of
competition. In particular:
a. there is no overlap in the brands of cars supplied by
Northstar and Mildura Mazda
b. there is a small horizontal overlap between Northstar and
Mildura Mazda in the supply of new and used motor vehicles
in the Mildura region
c. there are alternative suppliers of new and used cars in the
Mildura region.
The ACCC has also had regard to the likelihood that, if the
Acquisition were put into effect, the notification thresholds
determined under section 51ABP(1) of the Act would apply. The
ACCC considers that there is a low likelihood of the Acquisition
meeting the thresholds for notification.
The ACCC considers that the determination is consistent with the
object of the Act and the interests of consumers in promoting
competition.
For more information about the ACCC’s approach to considering
notification waiver applications and to assessing competition
effects more generally, see the ACCC’s interim guidance on
notification waivers and merger assessment guidelines.

Autopact Property No. 4 Pty Ltd and Autopact Holdings Pty Ltd

4 Pty Ltd’s proposed acquisition of 100% freehold interest in the

finance and insurance, and servicing and parts.
The Property is currently leased to Ferntree Gully Auto Sales Pty Ltd,
another wholly owned subsidiary of Autopact Holdings Pty Ltd and

events.title: Notification waiver determination published
events.determination_commission_division: Determination made by Commissioner Williams pursuant to a delegation under section 25(1) of the Act

determination_table_content.details:
PSC Transport Insurance Brokers Pty Ltd (PSCT), which is a wholly
owned subsidiary (indirectly) of The Envest Group Pty Ltd (TEG),
applied for a notification waiver in respect of its proposed
acquisition of the business and assets of MA Insurance Brokers Pty
Ltd (MA), as described in the transaction documents provided as
part of the application (the Acquisition).

The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has
determined under section 51ABV(1)(a) of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) that the Acquisition is not required to be
notified.
18 June 2026

The acquirer, TEG, is an insurance and financial services distribution
group. Through its wholly and partially owned subsidiaries, TEG is
active in Australia and New Zealand in the supply of underwriting
products and services as managing general agents, insurance
broking services, wealth management and financial planning
products and services, and adjacent and ancillary services such as
Insurtech (technology to support the insurance industry), claims
management, workers compensation consulting and trade credit
risk.
TEG is part of the Ardonagh Group which is a UK based independent
insurance distribution platform and a global independent broker.
The target, MA, is a self-licensed general insurance broker. MA is
based in Brisbane, but services clients across Australia. MA brokers
a range of insurance products including general liability,
management liability, professional indemnity, directors and officers,
property insurance, workers compensation, plant and machinery,
marine insurance and business insurance, but is specialised in
commercial motor insurance (and related insurance classes).
The parties both supply general insurance and commercial motor
insurance broking services in Australia.

In making this notification waiver determination, the Australian
Competition and Consumer Commission (the ACCC) has considered
the information provided with the notification waiver application and
had regard to the factors in section 51ABV(2)(b) of the Competition
and Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) (the Act).
Based on the information currently before it, the ACCC considers that
the Acquisition is unlikely to give rise to any material lessening of
competition. In particular:
a. the parties’ combined share of supply of general insurance
broking services in Australia, including for commercial motor
insurance, is estimated to be low and the increase in share
arising from the Acquisition would be small
b. there are several alternative suppliers of general insurance
and commercial motor broking services in Australia.
The ACCC has also had regard to the likelihood that, if the
Acquisition were put into effect, the notification thresholds
determined under section 51ABP(1) of the Act would apply.
While the ACCC considers that the notification thresholds are likely
to be met, given that material competition concerns are unlikely to
arise, the ACCC has determined that the Acquisition is not required
to be notified.
The ACCC considers that the determination is consistent with the
object of the Act and the interests of consumers in promoting
competition.

effects more generally, see the ACCC’s interim guidance on

determination_table_content.details:

Plenitude S.p.A. Società Benefit (Plenitude) by way of a purchase of

Acquisition).

Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) that the Acquisition is not required to be
notified.
18 June 2026

The acquirer, Bolt, is an Italian joint stock company and investment
entity ultimately controlled by funds managed or advised by affiliates
of Ares, a global alternative investment manager. Bolt currently holds
approximately 20% of the share capital of Plenitude which it acquired
from Eni in June 2022. In Australia, Ares' energy interests are held
through Windlab Pty Ltd (Windlab), jointly owned with Federation
Asset Management.
Windlab's portfolio comprises wind development projects in National
Electricity Market regions of Queensland, NSW, Victoria, Tasmania
and Western Australia (NEM regions), as well as having a
development role for one project in Western Australia (which is its
only project outside of the NEM). Windlab's activities include the

Baya project in North Queensland and the Junction Rivers project in
New South Wales.
Plenitude is an integrated energy transition company currently solely
controlled by Eni S.p.A.. Globally, Plenitude is active in the production
of electricity from renewable sources, in the retail sale of electricity
and natural gas to both domestic and non-domestic customers, in
the electric mobility sector, and in the provision of energy efficiency
services.
In Australia, Plenitude's activities are limited to utility-scale solar
generation on the Northern Territory's Darwin–Katherine
Interconnected System (outside the NEM regions). Through wholly-
owned Australian subsidiaries, Plenitude owns the Katherine solar
farm, the Batchelor solar farm and the Manton Dam solar farm with
45 MWAC contracted to the Northern Territory's government-owned
electricity retailer Jacana Energy.

had regard to the factors in section 51ABV(2)(b) of the Competition

lessening of competition. In particular, there is no geographic
horizontal overlap between Ares, its portfolio companies and
Plenitude in the supply of electricity generation in Australia.
The ACCC has also had regard to the likelihood that, if the

determined under section 51ABP(1) of the Act would apply.

to be met, given that material competition concerns are unlikely to
arise, the ACCC has determined that the Acquisition is not required
to be notified.
